title: Speciation — how new species form
examTip: Speciation links directly to natural selection, so AQA often tests them together. The key mechanism to explain is geographic isolation → separate gene pools → different selection pressures → different mutations accumulate → reproductive isolation = new species. Know what a species is (can interbreed to produce fertile offspring) and why isolation is essential.
introTips: Speciation is what happens when natural selection goes on long enough in two isolated groups — they end up so different they can no longer breed together.
The definition of a species is the key starting point: members of the same species can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Once that's impossible, you have two species.
The process always involves isolation first — you'll need to explain why the groups stop mixing and what happens differently to each group after that.
intro: Every species alive today evolved from an ancestor species. When a population becomes split into two isolated groups, natural selection starts working on each group independently. Over thousands of generations, the groups can become so different that even if they came back together, they couldn't breed successfully. That's speciation — and it's how life's extraordinary diversity came to exist.

keyPoints.heading: What is a species?
keyPoints.content: A species is a group of organisms that can interbreed to produce fertile offspring. Members of the same species share a common gene pool and can exchange genetic material through reproduction. The 'fertile offspring' part is crucial — a mule (offspring of a horse and a donkey) is not a new species because it is sterile and cannot reproduce. When two populations can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring, they are considered two separate species.
writeBullets: Species = group of organisms that can interbreed to produce fertile offspring
Fertile offspring = offspring that can themselves reproduce
Mule (horse × donkey) = sterile → not a new species
If two populations can no longer produce fertile offspring together → they are different species

keyTerms.def: A group of organisms that can interbreed with each other to produce fertile offspring
Offspring that are capable of reproducing themselves — key criterion for classifying organisms as the same species

keyPoints.memTip: Species = can breed + fertile offspring. Two criteria, both needed. The 'fertile' part catches most people — a horse and donkey produce a mule, but it's sterile, so they're still two different species.

keyPoints.heading: Geographic isolation — the starting point of speciation
keyPoints.content: Speciation almost always begins with geographic isolation: a physical barrier (mountain range, river, sea, desert) separates a population into two groups that can no longer interbreed. Once isolated, the two populations have separate gene pools. Each population then experiences different selection pressures (different predators, different food sources, different climate). Random mutations arise in both populations independently, and natural selection favours different alleles in each environment. Over many generations, the two populations accumulate different genetic differences and adapt in different directions.
writeBullets: Geographic isolation: physical barrier separates population → two groups can't interbreed
Separate gene pools: mutations arise independently in each group
Different selection pressures: different environments favour different alleles
Natural selection works differently on each population
Over generations, genetic differences accumulate in each group

keyTerms.def: The separation of a population by a physical barrier, preventing interbreeding and creating separate gene pools
All the alleles of all the genes in a population at a given time
An environmental factor (predation, disease, food availability, climate) that determines which individuals survive and reproduce

keyPoints.memTip: Isolation → separate gene pools → different mutations → different selection pressures → different adaptations. Draw it as a timeline: one population → barrier appears → two groups → each evolves separately → can't breed = speciation. The barrier is always the trigger.

keyPoints.heading: Reproductive isolation and the formation of a new species
keyPoints.content: After many generations of separate evolution, the two populations may become so genetically different that even if the barrier is removed, they can no longer successfully interbreed — or if they do, they produce infertile offspring. This is called reproductive isolation. At this point, the two groups are considered separate species. Reproductive isolation can arise through: different mating seasons, different courtship behaviours, physical incompatibility, or hybrid offspring being infertile. The key test is always: can they produce fertile offspring together? If not, they're separate species.
writeBullets: Reproductive isolation: even if barrier removed, two populations cannot produce fertile offspring
Causes: different mating seasons, different courtship behaviours, physical incompatibility, infertile hybrids
At point of reproductive isolation → two separate species have formed
Key test: can they produce fertile offspring? No = different species

keyTerms.def: When two populations can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring, even if they come into contact — the defining point of speciation
The formation of a new species, resulting from populations evolving in isolation until they become reproductively isolated from each other

keyPoints.memTip: The sequence: Geographic isolation → separate evolution → Reproductive isolation → New species. Geographic isolation is physical (mountain, sea). Reproductive isolation is biological (can't breed successfully). Reproductive isolation = the finish line of speciation.

keyPoints.heading: Evidence for evolution and speciation
keyPoints.content: Several lines of evidence support the theory of evolution and speciation. Fossil record: fossils show gradual changes in species over time and the existence of ancestor species that no longer exist. DNA and protein comparisons: closely related species have very similar DNA sequences; the more similar the DNA, the more recently the species diverged from a common ancestor. Comparative anatomy: similar underlying bone structures (e.g. human arm, dolphin flipper, bat wing — all have the same bones arranged differently) suggest common ancestry. AQA also expects you to know about the work of Darwin and Wallace in developing the theory of natural selection.
writeBullets: Fossil record: shows gradual change over time; evidence of ancestor species
DNA comparisons: more similar DNA = more closely related = diverged more recently
Protein comparisons: similar protein sequences indicate common ancestry
Comparative anatomy: similar bone structures in different species suggest common ancestor
Darwin and Wallace both independently developed natural selection theory

keyTerms.def: The collection of preserved remains or traces of organisms from the past, showing how life has changed over time
A species from which two or more later species both descended
The comparison of body structures across different species to identify evolutionary relationships

keyPoints.memTip: Three evidence types: Fossils (show change over time), DNA/proteins (show relatedness), Anatomy (similar structures = common ancestor). The closer the DNA match, the more recently two species diverged from a common ancestor.

tables.title: Steps in speciation
rows: Physical barrier splits one population into two
Each group has mutations independently; no gene flow between groups
Different environments favour different alleles in each group
Populations accumulate different genetic differences over generations
Groups can no longer produce fertile offspring together

0.question: What is the biological definition of a species?
options: A group of organisms that look similar and live in the same habitat
A group of organisms that can interbreed to produce fertile offspring
A group of organisms with identical DNA sequences
A group of organisms that eat the same food and have the same predators
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — 'interbreed to produce fertile offspring' is the precise AQA definition. Both parts matter: interbreeding AND fertile offspring.
0.wrongFeedback: The biological definition of a species is a group of organisms that can interbreed with each other to produce fertile offspring. Appearance, habitat, and DNA can vary within a species, and organisms from different species can sometimes mate (but their offspring are usually infertile). The 'fertile offspring' part is what defines the species boundary.

2.question: Why is geographic isolation necessary for speciation?
options: It causes mutations to occur at a faster rate in the isolated population
It prevents interbreeding, creating separate gene pools so each population evolves independently
It directly causes reproductive isolation by changing the organisms' DNA
It reduces food competition, allowing more individuals to survive and reproduce
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — without isolation, genes flow freely between populations and prevent divergence. Isolation creates separate gene pools so each population can evolve differently.
2.wrongFeedback: Geographic isolation is needed because it stops the two populations from interbreeding and exchanging genes. Once isolated, each group has its own separate gene pool — mutations arise independently and natural selection works on each group separately. Without isolation, genes flow between populations and stop them diverging enough to become separate species.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the 'fertile' part of the species definition — organisms from different species can sometimes mate and produce offspring (e.g. horse × donkey = mule), but those offspring are sterile. 'Fertile offspring' is what matters.
Saying speciation happens because organisms 'want to' or 'decide to' adapt — natural selection has no direction or intention. Mutations are random; selection acts on the variation that exists.

Confusing geographic isolation with reproductive isolation — geographic isolation is the physical barrier (mountain, river); reproductive isolation is the biological result (can no longer produce fertile offspring together). You need both in a speciation explanation.

Saying 'the DNA mutates faster in isolation' — geographic isolation doesn't speed up mutation. It prevents gene flow between populations so that independently-arising mutations lead to different adaptations.
Stopping the explanation at 'they became different' — always end with the reproductive isolation criterion: they can no longer produce fertile offspring, therefore they are now two species.
examTips: A full speciation answer for 4+ marks typically needs: (1) isolation prevents interbreeding, (2) separate gene pools / mutations arise independently, (3) different selection pressures / natural selection favours different alleles, (4) genetic differences accumulate, (5) can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring = new species.
When asked to 'define species', write both parts: 'can interbreed' AND 'to produce fertile offspring'. Half definitions lose marks.

If given a novel scenario (e.g. a population of birds separated by a new volcano), apply the standard framework: isolation → separate gene pools → different selection → genetic divergence → reproductive isolation → new species.
Evidence for evolution questions: list fossils, DNA comparisons, protein comparisons, comparative anatomy. The more you list and briefly explain, the more marks you get.
revisionCardBullets: Species = group of organisms that interbreed to produce fertile offspring
Mule = horse × donkey = sterile → horse and donkey are still different species
Speciation: population split by geographic isolation → separate gene pools
Separate gene pools → different mutations → different selection pressures
Natural selection acts differently on each population → different adaptations
Reproductive isolation: can no longer produce fertile offspring → two species formed
Evidence for evolution: fossil record, DNA/protein comparisons, comparative anatomy
More similar DNA = more closely related = diverged more recently from common ancestor
Darwin and Wallace independently developed natural selection theory
